Technical Specifications
PREPROCESSOR FOR ASSEMBLY CODE POLYMORPHING
PURPOSE
To develop a preprocessor that takes an Asm listing as input and outputs an Asm listing as output,
in which the code is equivalent to the source code, but differs in instructions and possibly other criteria.
REQUIREMENTS
The preprocessor should be implemented as a command-line utility.
I/O is at the developer's discretion:
- read from stdin and write the result to stdout
- command line keys from where to get the file and in which to put the answer
- combination of 1 and 2
There should be a brief help from the utility itself, and a detailed readme in Russian with a description of the program and its modes.
Any heuristics can be enabled and disabled by command line keys.
Any heuristic should have boundary conditions for its application - i.e. recommendations,
when it is safe and when it is not.
By default, only safe heuristics should be enabled.
The preprocessor must understand Microsoft Visual Studio 2010 assembly dialect.
It must work with both 32-bit and 64-bit instructions.
If you need to use compilers, translators and other tools, you should provide for decoupling from specific vendors and versions.
on specific vendors and versions.
If it is necessary to run a translator, it should be done by extension points.
But in general, it is assumed that the preprocessor will be included in one of the Visual Studio build events.
REALIZATION
The task is largely exploratory, and there are no ready-made recipes.
The following substitutions can be suggested:
- augmenting and diluting the code with noise (meaningful instructions that do nothing)
- relocation of addresses that do not require relocation (or introducing an additional offset into addressing
by adding parasitic bytes in the code)
- a sub-option is changing the order of variable allocation on the stack
- moving a global to the stack and a stack variable to a global
- moving static data to the .text code section
- xor al, al to mov al, 0 and the like
- changing the order of functions and independent code sections (you may need to split one file into several)
- changing the order of data-independent instructions inside one function (if there are no memory barriers)
- changing the order of data definition

- encryption of functions with dynamic decryption: search for the prologue and epilogue of functions, encrypt the body, replace the body with a bunch of db <byte>, substitute the decryption code into the function prologue
Probably, the methods of making the code polymorphic should be based on the work done in the 90s.
Use getopt.c implementation for command line parsing.
